speaker: interviewer
text: Renee Klaristenfeld.

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I go on?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, dear.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you went shopping in the warehouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Then I went to-- in the factory, there was a German who was in charge of the factory, also in uniform. He was short and stocky. He looked a little bit like Churchill. And now he came over to me and was concerned how I am, and what he can do, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one time, he was saying to me that he had some handkerchiefs. And he is-- would love to have his initial embroidered. And I was very handy. So I offered to do it for him. And I remember, I did. But, also, he would come over during the night sometimes, and say, open your mouth. And he would pop a candy in my mouth, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he was quite nice to me, this one person. I have to say that life was not horrible there. I mean, it was, it was not good. We still had bunks. We had more food-- I remember having bread left over or food left over that I didn't, you know-- obviously, I wasn't a great eater. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was so much better than you felt already, there must have been some hope in our heart that things are going from very bad to a little bit better. [? And they'll ?] gradually.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was still with my sister. What happened, though, one night, my sister [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] felt very hot. We were laying next to each other. And she felt very, very hot. And I remember going down-- and again, it was very eerie, very dark, and very quiet. And I took a towel, or a cloth, whatever I had there. And I went somewhere where they had water to wring out this towel and put it on her head to cool her off. I could see that she is sick. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the next day, I told the German that she is sick. Now in, in Auschwitz, you might have been afraid to say it. But here, somehow-- number one, you didn't have much of a choice. I didn't want her to, to, you know, get sick enough to, to maybe die. So I told somebody that she is sick. And what was interesting in this particular city-- they actually took her to the hospital. They took her to a German hospital. I didn't see her for weeks. And then they returned her, healthy. And she was telling me that nuns were in charge in the hospital. And they treated her very kindly. During the time that she was in the hospital, we had our first air ride and bombing. And that was one of the nightmares. The first time, the first time it was-- we came home from a night shift. And it was an absolutely beautiful day. It was very sunny and very beautiful. I mean, the kind of day that you love to be alive. And then they were going to serve lunch. So you lined up outside. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I heard, like, a buzzing. And I said to somebody, look up in the sky. Look how beautiful, these little, tiny, little silver planes. They were like-- you know, like you see them, like in a triangle, almost, like maybe six or eight or 10 of them. And they looked so little, like toy planes. And I said, look how cute. I just thought it was so nice. And no sooner did I finish saying it than the bombs started falling.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, of course, everybody ran into the tent, didn't know where to run. And there was a bunker, a cement-built bunker, for one person, for the guard. There was, like, maybe two or three of them around the tent.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in that-- I have to say that the guard was an elderly man, elderly German, of course, also uniform. And he approached me many times, spoke very kindly told me, through the wire, before-- prior to this bombing. And he said to me, one time-- you look very German. You must have some German blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, no, I don't. And he said, but think back, maybe your grandparents. I said, no. We were always Jewish. And you know, like, I was very defiant. Like, I figured, whatever happens, I'll go out in style, you know? So during that bombing, we got absolutely panicky. And everybody started running. And I said that these barbed wires-- with just with bare hands, we pried them open and pushed through.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, all I could remember is everybody running somewhere. I just want to say that the, the group consisted of 200 girls when they took us from Auschwitz. So we lived-- these 200 girls lived together. And I pushed through. And I'm running to this bunker. The German is standing already outside. They pushed him out. There must have been a dozen girls in that bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I realized, OK. There is no room in the bunker. What to do? And in the meantime, you hear this whistling sound, with the bombs coming down and exploding.

speaker: interviewee
text: The tracks were right there. And there was a train, waiting, probably to come and go, or pick up the ammunition. And I made a decision. I'm going to lay down on the tracks, under the train. I don't want to see what's going to happen. Whatever has to happen-- I don't want to see it. I remember seeing. It was an open field. It was a beautiful afternoon. And I remember seeing a lot of the girls running, running, running. I could just see them running all over. I made my decision to lay down there and be protected by the train, which wasn't the wisest thing to do. Because their very purpose was to knock out the, the tracks, and not to give them a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this is where I felt comfortable. So I did it. And I remember you could hear the bombs falling when they were in the air. And I waited. Where is it going to fall? And when I heard it explode, and I was still there? So, you know-- like, I was grateful after each bomb fell that I was there. And, and I was still there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I remember a girl saw me there. And she came running. And she lay down next to me. And then, after a while, it was all over. And the Germans came by, said, OK, you can all come out. It's over. The raid is over. The bombing is over. And it was starting to get a little dark. And the Germans came in. And something very interesting happened, even though I have very little good things to say about the Germans and-- but this was interesting. And maybe it had to do with a town that wasn't as familiar with-- that you can just take people and gas them. Or, or they were more humane, I have to say. Because they came with ambulances.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came and made an announcement and said that anybody who was OK and is not wounded, should go out to the open field. There are a lot of wounded people. And I remember going and just thinking, I'm so grateful that my sister is not here so I don't have to worry about her, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came out. And I remember it was like a battlefield. Like, instead of soldiers, it was our girls laying there. And some of them were dead. And some of them were wounded. I remember some of them lost a limb, an arm, or a leg. And young Germans, that I know for a fact were not in charge in camp-- they came with stretchers. And they lifted them up. And they took them to hospitals. And they treated them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, that was the very first time. But it was the beginning. And it went on at night. And the whole city was lit up. I mean, it was unbelievable. And even though we, we were saying that-- good for the Germans, you know? We want to see them get what's due to them. But, at the same time, you didn't know what was going to happen to you next.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a while, I even remember, on one occasion-- even though the Germans made a statement. They made an announcement. They said, if it happens again, and it will, because they know they're going to come back, don't run out of the tent. Because they have information that you're prisoners. They're looking for soldiers, not you. If you run in the field, they're very high up. And they cannot see who is running. So stay inside and you'll be safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, you know, the bombs started falling, you ran. And I remember, once, I ran all the way into town, all the way into town. I've never seen the town before. And there was a big bunker with a lot of Germans hiding there during the bombing. And I ran in with them. And they were like, just, you know-- I remember by their expression, like, who is this, here? You know, I was-- didn't make the best impression. I wasn't dressed like them. And, you know? You were still [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Whether they knew where I'm from, I don't know. But they didn't say a word to me. They just sort of looked a little like, what are you doing here? And I don't recall how I found my way back. But obviously, there was no place else to go and so went back. And then the, the city was being bombed continuously, until they must have knocked out the whole factory. And they decided to move on with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister was back with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had brought back to my sister. You know something? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't even recall now. I really should ask my sister if it was there or-- if I am not mistaken, she caught up with us. They brought her to where they took us next. And, and from then on, it was really just wandering from town to town, from place to place. Wherever we went, there were bombings. We were once on a train. They stopped at the train station, on the tracks. And right next to us on the tracks was a wagons with German soldiers. And they started bombing right then and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember, we jumped out of the train. And it was like-- we were up, like, higher, on a hill. And we ran down. And nobody bothered us. They let us run.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, we were saying-- and then I was-- at that point, I was with my sister. And I remember saying, well, you know, nobody's bothering us. We could just walk into town and maybe escape. But you didn't know. You didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What am I going to say? Where am I from? Who am I, you know? And what are my chances? So we just went back. And then we went from town, to town, to town. And we were getting hungrier and tireder and no-- They weren't feeding you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little-- but the Germans were very down, I guess. They had information the war is not going well.

speaker: interviewer
text: When is this? When-- what time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this must have been-- when we left-- may-- before January. Because this booklet that I'm talking about-- where it was my birthday in January, we were already settled in another place that was called Sommerda.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so this was-- so this would have been by December of 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: It might have been around then. Everybody was very tired and very hungry. And people started to just fall in the street. And, and you-- they used to take wheelbarrows and just put in the person. And what they did, I don't know. I don't know. And my sister, I remember, started complaining that she can't make it. And she can't make it. And she can't make it. But before that, when they took us to Sommerda-- because I'm getting ahead of myself. I don't have it in proper sequence. I didn't get-- I didn't rehearse for this.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] You had said that you were on the trains. And they kept moving you from town to town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we, we arrived-- we arrived. Before they moved us town to town, I have to backtrack just for a moment. We went to this other camp-- a small town called Sommerda. And this is where it was my birthday, where my sister wrote this little poem. She had this artist-- would be interesting to hold it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. We can do that. Hold on one second. Can we stop for one second?

words.text: Can we stop for one second?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to have an idea. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This little book is made of cardboard paper. And they found the, a sheet of paper, and they cut it up. And they took a piece of rope and tied it together. And she wrote, Sommerda. And I think it says, January, 1945. It's, it's now, with the years, a little bit faded. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my sister-- the writing inside is very clear. I don't know. She must have got a pen from somewhere. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold it up and show us the writing. Can you read us some of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would need my glasses. I don't know where I put them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are these the glasses?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, these are my sunglasses. [INAUDIBLE] No, my sunglasses-- I had another pair of glasses. Maybe on the dining room table?

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop for a second. And we'll, we'll get your glasses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sommerda, 1945, January 26. Dear Renee, this is the occasion of your birthday. And I will attempt-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, it's a very politely phrased-- you know, the Hungarians had this very-- I will attempt, and be careful, and with patience, what I say so that I will not make a mistake, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and, and that I shouldn't wish you everything good. In other words, I should-- I will be very careful in making sure that I express all the good wishes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I say it from my heart. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My heart is as clean as the snow. I would have liked to get you a gift, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like I used to every year. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It is true that it used to be different then, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] when my mother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] would, would love, would give you a kiss. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you would get a nice gift. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And everybody would wish you a lot of happiness. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would have-- who-- OK. Just a minute. Who would have dared think that in one year, things would change to this extent, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that whoever you were dearest to, you are now separated from? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You were sent away from home with your sister, somewhere far away, in some camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In one year, so much suffering, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] so much tear, so many tears, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] sleepy and tired, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and always in your memory, something pleasant from your home, that you remember something pleasant from your home. Our dear parents and sisters, where are they? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Most likely, their tears are also-- [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] but, unfortunately, there is nothing we can do about it. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Even though it's so difficult [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] that we have to suffer, we have to suffer with patience. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we are-- but we are Jewish children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we always believed in God, and still believe. So we have-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we have to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wish I could think of the word-- accept-- OK. We have to accept that this was His will. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Now that it's your birthday, and it's almost simultaneously with mine-- because hers was, like, two weeks later-- I plead with God that he should give us this gift together, [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] that He should watch our our parents-- our dear parents and our sisters. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And as suddenly as we were-- as suddenly as it happened that we were separated, that He will bring us back together again. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I know it's a little difficult now. But don't lose faith. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's a little cloudy, but it will clear up. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] No-- it's a little cloudy now, but next birthday will be that much clearer. There will come a day when the sun will shine for us. And, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] and the gate of the camps will open for us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we, in our joy, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] will yell, we are free. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And because you had to suffer when you were so young, God will like you that much more, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that you will never face adversity, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and that you will be happy till you're 120.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she made that for you. And who decorated it like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a woman artist, I mentioned. There was a woman who was very artistic. As a matter of fact, she survived the war. And she was an artist. She did very beautiful paintings. And the two of them surprised me with this. Take it off?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, to see your beautiful face.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a birthday--

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was my birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your 15th birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in Sommerda.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How long were you there in Sommerda?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there, we were there for a while. We lived in barracks. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- we also went to work. We went to work. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same type of work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Munitions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember. There was a lady that looked like a very old lady to me. She was in our, in our barracks, and a, a religious lady. And she would sit. And, and the girls would circle around her. And she remembered the prayers by heart. And she would say them. And we would repeat them. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was a very good worker. I, I didn't mind the work. But I didn't want to be punished. So I was always attending. But it was Yom Kippur evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, are you going forward, or back? You must be going back.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going forward.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this was 1945. In '45, Yom, Yom Kippur-- you wouldn't have been in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you know. You are so right.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. So you're going back now.

speaker: interviewee
text: So why-- should have made notes so that I don't get it all confused.

speaker: interviewer
text: It doesn't matter. That's what I'm here for.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Good. I'm glad you're so smart. OK. It was Yom Kippur. And I was in Sommerda. And I don't know. I may have, like you're, you're pointing out-- I may have run ahead of myself a little bit. But, anyhow, the story's the same. It was Yom Kippur. And I don't have to tell you what that means to us. And what it meant at home. So I remember, I got some sort of a little-- it was a cut. It was really, sort of an abrasive something on the foot, when you step down on the foot. And it really bothered me a little. Not enough to make a great fuss about it, but it was a little painful. And it was Yom Kippur, when you started the fast. And I had a day shift at the time. And it bothered me terribly. I mean, I knew I was going to fast. But it bothered me very much that I would have to go to work. And so I decided to, to play a game and see if it works out. I came out. They lined us up to go to work. And I came out with one shoe on. And I was hobbling on that one foot. And I remember there was a German there that was so handsome, so young, and so mean, and so rotten. It was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says to me, where's the other shoe? I can't put it on. What's the problem? I have a very bad sore on the bottom of my foot. It's very, very painful. And I cannot put it on. Well, are you sure? Have you tried?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I said, you know. I never stay out from work. I don't mind working. I'm a very good worker. I always go. I never look for an excuse. But this is the truth. This is just too painful. And I cannot put my shoe on. And I cannot step down. OK, so go back to the barrack. And if I would win the Lotto with $20 million today, I wouldn't feel as happy, having accomplished that, that I didn't have to go to work that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: On Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewee
text: On Yom Kippur.
